title: The Agent With the Wrong Keys
summary: A new AI data analyst keeps answering questions about payroll data nobody authorized it to see. Its execution role was generated by a wizard.

glossary.Amazon Bedrock AgentCore: A managed runtime for hosting AI agents on AWS. Each agent runtime has an IAM execution role (a machine identity) that the agent uses for every AWS API call it makes.
glossary.Execution role: An IAM role attached to a compute resource (here, the AgentCore Runtime) that supplies temporary credentials whenever the workload calls an AWS API. The permissions on this role are the permissions the agent has.
glossary.AgentCore starter toolkit: An open-source convenience library that scaffolds a working agent in a few commands. It includes an auto-create-role helper that generates an execution role when one is not supplied.
glossary.Principle of least privilege: A security practice that says a workload should have exactly the permissions it needs and nothing more. For AI agents that can decide what tools to call, least privilege is the strongest line of defense.
glossary.Resource ARN: An Amazon Resource Name is the unique identifier for an AWS resource. Scoping a policy to a specific ARN (for example one bucket) limits the action to that resource; using a wildcard (*) means any resource of that type in the account.
glossary.aws:SourceAccount condition: An IAM policy condition that requires the caller to be inside a specific AWS account. It is a common guardrail for trust policies to prevent another account from assuming the role.
glossary.CloudTrail: The AWS audit log. It records every API call including who made it, when, from where, and on what resource. The caller identity is the first thing to look at when investigating unauthorized data access.
glossary.Data perimeter: A set of IAM and Service Control Policy guardrails that ensure only trusted identities access trusted resources from trusted networks. It catches over-permissioned identities before they do damage.

system.what_broke: The brackish-data-insights-agent AgentCore execution role was created by the starter toolkit's auto-create-role helper. The generated inline policy grants s3:*, kms:Decrypt, and secretsmanager:GetSecretValue with Resource: "*". The agent, which was only supposed to answer questions about the analytics bucket, started listing and reading objects from the payroll and customer-PII buckets because its tool-use loop decided those buckets might contain useful context.
system.data_flow: A business user asks a question in the web app. The Lambda router forwards it to the AgentCore agent. The agent plans, chooses an S3 list_objects tool call, picks a bucket based on the question (including buckets outside its scope when the question is ambiguous), reads matching files, summarizes, and answers. CloudTrail records every GetObject and ListBucket call with the agent's execution role as the caller identity.

components.name: brackish-data-insights-agent (AgentCore Runtime)
components.role: B2B data enrichment assistant. Answers questions about customer segmentation and campaign performance from the analytics data lake.
connections: Invoked by the Lambda chat router
Uses execution role brackish-data-insights-agent-role for every AWS API call
Reads from the intended analytics bucket brackish-analytics-reports
components.failure_impact: If the execution role is over-permissioned, the agent can read data outside its intended scope. The agent's tool-use loop may choose to do so autonomously.

components.name: brackish-data-insights-agent-role (IAM role)
components.role: Execution role used by the AgentCore Runtime for every AWS API call it makes. Grants the permissions the agent is allowed to use.
connections: Trust policy allows bedrock-agentcore.amazonaws.com to assume
Inline policy attached with overly broad S3/KMS/Secrets permissions
components.failure_impact: A wildcard Resource: * on s3:* means the agent can read, write, and delete objects in any bucket in the account. A compromise or a misuse of the agent is a compromise of every bucket.

components.name: brackish-prod-trail (CloudTrail)
components.role: Logs all management events for the AWS account, plus S3 data events for the payroll and customer-PII buckets.
connections: Records every GetObject on the two sensitive buckets
components.failure_impact: Without it there is no way to know which buckets the agent touched. Because it is on, the investigation is possible at all.

components.name: brackish-analytics-reports, brackish-payroll-exports, brackish-customer-pii (S3 buckets)
components.role: The first bucket is the one the agent is supposed to read. The other two are owned by other teams and were never intended to be visible to the agent.
connections: All three are in the same AWS account
Bucket policies rely on IAM identity-based permissions rather than resource-based allowlists
components.failure_impact: Payroll and PII buckets are Tier 1 data under SOC 2 controls; unintended reads are a reportable security event.

progressive_clues: The agent is giving accurate answers. What makes you sure it is only reading the buckets it is supposed to?
Who is the Principal in the access logs for the payroll bucket? What role is it using?
How was the agent's execution role created, and what does its inline policy say about Resource?
A Resource of * on s3:* means every bucket in the account is in scope, not just the ones the agent was designed for.
The AgentCore starter toolkit's auto-create-role helper generates these permissions by default. The docs warn it is for development only, not production.

resolution.root_cause: The AgentCore execution role brackish-data-insights-agent-role has an inline policy granting s3:*, kms:Decrypt, and secretsmanager:GetSecretValue with Resource: "*". The role was generated by the AgentCore starter toolkit's auto-create-role helper, which scopes permissions broadly for development convenience. No one replaced it with a production-scoped role before the agent went live. The agent's tool-use loop reads from any S3 bucket it decides might be relevant.

fix_criteria.description: Player identifies that the agent is reading from buckets outside its intended scope (payroll and PII) by correlating S3 access logs or CloudTrail events with the agent's execution role ARN
Player identifies that the execution role inline policy uses Resource: * for s3:*, kms:*, and secretsmanager:*
Player traces the role back to the AgentCore starter toolkit's auto-generated default
Player proposes a replacement policy that scopes s3:Get* and s3:ListBucket to the analytics bucket ARNs only, and removes kms:* and secretsmanager:* unless explicitly needed
Player proposes a Service Control Policy or Resource Control Policy that prevents any agent role from reading the payroll or PII buckets regardless of identity policy

learning_objectives: How IAM execution roles give AgentCore agents their effective permissions, and why wildcards are especially dangerous for agents that choose their own tools
How to read CloudTrail and S3 access logs to identify unauthorized data access by a machine identity
How the AgentCore starter toolkit's auto-create-role helper is intended for development and why production deployments need a scoped role
How Service Control Policies, Resource Control Policies, and bucket-level guardrails combine to form a data perimeter
sop_steps: Pin down what the agent actually touched. Open CloudTrail and filter for the agent's execution role ARN (arn:aws:iam::ACCOUNT:role/brackish-data-insights-agent-role) over the last seven days. Group the events by bucket name and API action. Any bucket the agent was not supposed to read is evidence.

Read the agent's execution role inline policy. Run aws iam get-role-policy --role-name brackish-data-insights-agent-role --policy-name <inline-policy-name> and look at the Resource field for every Action. If any statement has Resource: "*" for Action like s3:* or kms:Decrypt, you have found the root cause.

Find how the role was created. Look at the role's Tags and its CreateDate. The AgentCore starter toolkit adds a tag aws:bedrock-agentcore:role-source or similar, and the description starts with "Auto-generated by bedrock-agentcore-starter-toolkit". The AWS documentation for this toolkit explicitly says the default role is for development and not for production.

Stop the bleeding. Attach a deny policy to the role that explicitly denies s3:Get*, s3:List*, s3:Put* on the two sensitive buckets' ARNs. A deny statement overrides any allow, so this is fast and safe. Do this before you write the replacement allow policy.

Write a least-privilege replacement policy. Scope s3:GetObject and s3:ListBucket to exactly the bucket ARNs the agent is supposed to read, for example arn:aws:s3:::brackish-analytics-reports and arn:aws:s3:::brackish-analytics-reports/*. Remove kms:*, remove secretsmanager:*, and any other wildcard action. If the agent needs to decrypt data, scope kms:Decrypt to the specific KMS key ARN for that bucket.

Swap the policy atomically. Use aws iam put-role-policy to replace the inline policy in one call. Then remove the temporary deny once the new allow is in place and verified. Test with a known-good question to confirm the agent still works for its intended use case.

Add IAM Access Analyzer on the account. Access Analyzer flags roles with permissions broader than they are actually using. It would have caught this role before the incident. Set up notifications to a Slack channel or email the security team checks daily.

Add a data perimeter control. Write a Resource Control Policy (or a bucket policy on the payroll and PII buckets) that allows access only from specific identity ARNs in a specific Organizational Unit. This way even if another role is accidentally over-permissioned, the sensitive buckets are protected by a resource-level allowlist, not just identity-level least privilege.

Rotate credentials and rerun the audit. Rotate any KMS keys or secrets the over-permissioned agent could have decrypted. Recheck CloudTrail data events for the last 30 days to confirm the scope of access and write up a customer-notifiable incident timeline if the policy technically granted read access to regulated data.

related_failure_modes.scenario: The execution role uses a managed policy like AdministratorAccess.
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: Instead of a broad inline policy, an AWS-managed policy such as AdministratorAccess is attached directly to the role. The failure mode is essentially the same (the agent can do anything the account can do) but harder to miss in a policy review because AdministratorAccess is a named, well-known policy.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Forbid attaching AdministratorAccess to any agent role at the organizational level via an SCP. Require all agent roles to use inline or customer-managed policies reviewed by the security team. Access Analyzer External Access can flag roles with administrator-level privileges.

related_failure_modes.scenario: The role is correctly scoped, but the bucket policy grants any identity in the account s3:GetObject.
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The identity-based policy is fine, but the bucket has a resource-based policy like Principal: {"AWS": "arn:aws:iam::ACCOUNT:root"} with s3:GetObject on the bucket. Any role, including a new agent role, inherits read access as soon as it exists in the account. The agent's own role looks innocent in isolation.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Treat resource-based allow-all policies as dangerous. Use explicit principal lists on bucket policies, or use AWS Organizations Resource Control Policies to require specific identity ARNs or a specific aws:PrincipalOrgID for access.

related_failure_modes.scenario: The agent's role is scoped correctly, but a tool handler assumes a separate highly privileged role to do its work.
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The execution role on the AgentCore Runtime is perfectly scoped. But one of the tools the agent can call is a Lambda function that does sts:AssumeRole into a more privileged role before calling S3. From the outside it looks like the agent's role is safe; in reality the agent has effective access to whatever that secondary role allows.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Treat every transitive role chain as part of the agent's effective policy. Review tool-handler code for AssumeRole calls. Prefer passing restricted temporary credentials from the agent directly, and keep the assume-role chain as short and auditable as possible.

sop_practices: Never ship an agent to production with a starter-toolkit-generated execution role. The AWS documentation for the AgentCore starter toolkit explicitly says the auto-generated role is for development only. Write a scoped role as part of your agent's infrastructure-as-code template.

Enable IAM Access Analyzer on every account that hosts AgentCore agents. Its unused-access findings catch over-permissioned roles before an incident. Review findings weekly and convert them into backlog tickets.

Build a data perimeter with Service Control Policies and Resource Control Policies. Identity least privilege alone is not enough; a resource-level allowlist on sensitive buckets means a new role in the account cannot accidentally read regulated data no matter what its own policy says.

Treat agent tool loops as autonomous. Unlike a traditional service, an AI agent chooses its own API calls based on natural-language input. That makes every permission in its role reachable by a creative prompt. The safer posture is not "is this permission needed?" but "is this permission safe to grant to a process that chooses its own calls?"
